---
title: "Electrifying Australia's Diesel Fleet"
subtitle: "From diesel replacement priorities to the Sydney–Melbourne corridor"
author: "David Leitch"
date: 2026-03-24
image: ../media/image-20260322160659133.png
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    embed-resources: true
    smaller: false
    scrollable: true
    transition: slide
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    include-after-body: nav_back.html
---

# Part 1: Prioritising Diesel Replacement {background-color="#2c3e50" style="color: white;"}

## The prize

- Full electrification redirects **~$22 bn/yr** from petroleum imports to domestic electricity (~1% of GDP)
- **90 Mt CO2** reduction (~15% of national emissions)
- At AER shadow price of $176/t CO2 → ~$15 bn in welfare gains
- NPV at 5% bond rate: **~$750 bn**

## Three priority sectors

![](../media/image-20260322160659133.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

## Policy scorecard

![](../media/diesel_policy_scorecard_table.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

::: {.notes}
Ranks segments by diesel volume, electrification economics, and vehicle availability. Heavy artics, mining, and diesel passenger cars score highest.
:::

## Road diesel breakdown

![](../media/diesel_drilldown_2_road_trucks_lcv.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

## Diesel segment profiles

![](../media/diesel_segment_reference_table.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

## Commodity freight

![](../media/commodity_freight_chart.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

## Diesel replacement economics

![](../media/image-20260322132527116.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

::: {.notes}
At retail prices, 100% diesel replacement saves ~$54 bn/yr. Import substitution saving is ~$22 bn/yr. Plus $15 bn in avoided CO2 → $37 bn/yr total, ~2% of GDP.
:::

## Electricity demand for full replacement

![](../media/image-20260322132644147.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

::: {.notes}
~84 TWh required — about 30% of current Australian electricity supply. Substantial but achievable given renewable energy additions in the past decade.
:::

## Key policy lever: Fuel Tax Credits

- FTCS cost **$7.8 bn** in FY 2022–23 (larger than Commonwealth hospital spend)
- Mining claims **45%** ($3.5 bn) — coal miners alone ~$1.7 bn/yr
- Reduces the fuel cost advantage of electrification by ~30%
- Reform options: phase-down for mining, electrification offset, carbon-linked reduction

# Part 2: Sydney to Melbourne {background-color="#2c3e50" style="color: white;"}

## The case: a no brainer

- **22 Mt/yr** of freight travels the 900 km corridor (~1.2 bn freight-km)
- Full electrification saves **~$0.9 bn/yr** in fuel costs
- Total capex to electrify: ~$4 bn → payback **under 5 years**
- Incremental capex (charging + vehicle premium): ~$2.5 bn → payback **under 3 years**

## Electric freight cost comparison

![](../media/syd_mel_electrification-2748515-2748517.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

## The B-Double

![](../media/bdouble_front_trailer_outline-2670384.jpg){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

- 60 tonne B-double with 700 kWh battery
- One charging stop Sydney–Melbourne, charge takes ~1 hour
- Every electric km costs **1/3** the diesel cost

## Running cost comparison

|                 | Electric (1.9 kWh/km) | Diesel (50 L/100km) |
| --------------- | -------------------- | ------------------ |
| Energy rate     | $0.15/kWh            | $2.00/L            |
| Energy cost/km  | $0.285               | $1.00              |
| Service cost/km | $0.014               | $0.031             |
| **Running cost/km** | **$0.30**        | **$1.03**          |

**Every km: $0.70 cheaper than diesel**

## China is already doing it

![](../media/image-20260227113010480.png){fig-align="center" width="85%"}

::: {.notes}
~200,000 electric semi-trailers sold per year in China. 9,000+ public charging stations for heavy-duty electric trucks. Electric heavy truck penetration reached 28.9% of new sales in 2025.
:::

## The tech exists now

- **CATL**: 1.5 million km battery warranty
- **Windrose E1400**: 670 km range, 68t B-double, MW charging capable
- **Megawatt Charging Standard (MCS)**: charge 700 kWh in ~1 hour
- **Huawei 100 MW hub**: 126 bays, US$21M, 700 trucks/day

## Other benefits

- **Carbon**: ~1.7 Mt CO2/yr avoided on this corridor alone
- **Driver welfare**: less noise, no diesel fumes, better cab design
- **Time savings**: 30–60 min faster each way (electric power uphill)
- **Road safety**: all vehicles at speed limit on hills
- **Energy security**: reduced oil import dependence

## What's needed

1. **Charging infrastructure** on the Hume Highway — 6–12 purpose-built stations
2. **Grid connections** at logistics hubs (Moorebank, Somerton)
3. **Standards alignment** — MCS connectors and 1,250V DC systems
4. **Policy nudge** — the economics are compelling, but capital needs to be mobilised

## {background-color="#2c3e50" style="color: white;"}

::: {style="text-align: center; font-size: 1.5em; margin-top: 15%;"}
**The economics are outstanding.**

**The need is obvious.**

**An absolute no brainer.**
:::

## {background-color="#f5f5f5"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 20%;"}
### Thank you

Read the full articles and more at

[**itkservices3.com**](https://itkservices3.com){style="font-size: 1.3em;"}

[Return to site →](/)
:::
